title: Correlated Quantum Sensing at the Seemingly Classical Limit
authors: K. P. Athulya, Sreenath K. Manikandan
abstract: It is a difficult task to detect the indivisible quanta of weakly interacting radiation fields, and even more challenging to probe their quantum statistics. Nevertheless, if barely functional high-quality resonant detectors are feasible for weakly interacting radiation fields, they do come with certain statistical advantages to probe quantum effects at the seemingly classical limit of a large number of quanta of the incoming radiation field. We present correlated counting, homodyne, and heterodyne detection strategies using high-quality resonant quantum harmonic detectors operating at this limit, initialized in bolometry-inspired zero-mean preparations such as thermal states. We compare the bolometric regime of good resonant harmonic detectors in quantum optics to the bolometric regime of barely functional resonant mass quadrupole oscillators as detectors for quantum gravity. Simple statistical tests are proposed using symmetric correlators for two and three such barely functional resonant mass detectors that could reveal the complementary quantum noise characteristics of gravitons in tabletop experiments.
summary: This paper details advanced quantum sensing techniques to detect faint quantum fields, such as gravitons, by analyzing correlations across multiple detectors. It establishes that correlated measurements offer a statistical advantage over single-detector measurements, particularly in the weak coupling regime. The methodology involves deriving complex cross-correlators for counting and quadrature measurements, providing a theoretical roadmap for tabletop quantum gravity experiments.
main_problem: The core problem is developing methods to detect the quanta of weakly interacting radiation fields, such as gravitons, by probing their quantum statistics, especially when the field is in a seemingly classical limit ($\langle N angle \gg 1$).
main_result: The authors show that correlated quantum sensing strategies, utilizing cross-correlators ($\kappa_2, \kappa_3$) or quadrature measurements (Homodyne/Heterodyne), provide a unique statistical advantage for detecting quantum noise even when detectors are only 'barely functional' (weakly coupled bolometric regime).
method: The analysis employs quantum field theory techniques, calculating expectation values and correlators using the Glauber-Sudarshan P representation for multi-detector setups.
model_or_system: The system involves coupling a quantized radiation field (photons or gravitons) to one or more resonant quantum harmonic detectors (e.g., mass quadrupole oscillators). The interaction is modeled via a time-dependent Hamiltonian, analyzed in the weak coupling limit ($\gamma_0 	au \ll 1$).
key_observables: Cross-correlators ($\kappa_2, \kappa_3$), mean number of detected quanta ($ ar{n}$), number fluctuations ($\Delta n^2$), and quadrature correlations ($\langle x_1 x_2 angle - \langle x_1 angle \langle x_2 angle$).
important_parameters: The critical regime is the weak coupling bolometric limit where $\gamma_0 	au \ll 1$ but the product $\gamma_0 	au \langle N angle \sim 1$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes Markovian evolution and that the detectors are initialized in zero-mean preparations, such as thermal states.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares cross-correlated quantum sensing across three regimes: quantum optics, and resonant mass detectors for gravitational waves (GW).
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops detection strategies by comparing correlated counting, homodyne, and heterodyne measurements for $k=2$ and $k=3$ detectors, deriving the necessary correlators for each scheme.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems because it extends the concept of quantum noise measurement into the challenging regime of weak coupling and high photon numbers, providing concrete statistical tools for experimental proposals.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Cross-correlated quantum sensing using two or more high-quality, but barely functional resonant quantum harmonic detectors in the bolometric regime can offer a statistical advantage for probing the coherence properties of weak fields in the seemingly classical limit of large ⟨N⟩. We compare (b) the bolometric regime of good resonant harmonic detectors for probing quantum aspects of light to (c) the bolometric regime of barely functional resonant mass detectors for probing the quantum nature of gravitational radiation. The comparison is made in the functional detector regime, i.e., γ0τ⟨N⟩∼1 for both cases.
